title: Sneak peek: Vibe coding AI agents course with Kaggle
summary: Google Cloud Tech and Kaggle are offering a 5-day intensive course on building autonomous AI agents using vibe coding, a natural language-based programming approach. The course covers agent workflows, agent-to-agent communication, external API integrations, and includes a capstone project called Kaggriculture where participants deploy agents to compete.
writeup: This video is the orientation session for the fourth cohort of Google Cloud and Kaggle's intensive '5 Days of Vibe Coding Agents' course, hosted by Brenda Flynn, Anant Nowalgaria, and Smitha Kolan. The session traces the course's origin to late 2022, when Anant recognized that a static book on LLMs and embeddings would be outdated before print, and walks through the stack of agent tooling Google shipped at Cloud Next and Google I/O: Antigravity 2.0, ADK 2.0, a Managed Agents API, and over 50 Google-managed MCP servers. Kaggle's role as the community backbone is also framed, covering its 31-million-member base, Game Arena for head-to-head model benchmarking, and a public benchmark platform where researchers author evaluation tasks without managing infrastructure.

key_points: Google launched Antigravity 2.0 as a standalone desktop platform for orchestrating multiple agents in parallel, scheduling background tasks, and integrating natively with Google AI Studio, Android, and Firebase, alongside an Antigravity CLI optimized for terminal speed.

ADK 2.0 (Agent Development Kit) ships a unified graph-based engine with a slider from dynamic model-driven reasoning to strict deterministic workflows, adds ADK Kotlin in beta so Android mobile agents can coordinate with back-end Python agents, and is described as 'engineering first, low floor, high ceiling.'

The Managed Agents API lets developers spin up an agent that reasons, uses tools, and executes code in an isolated Linux environment with a single API call; it runs on Gemini 3.5 Flash and supports persistent, resumable sessions across calls.

Google announced more than 50 managed MCP servers, now generally available or in preview, covering BigQuery, Maps, Workspace, and Cloud Run, each with enterprise-grade security including model armor for prompt injection defense and built-in observability.
Kaggle's Game Arena launched chess as its first head-to-head model competition, using live model-vs-model play rather than static benchmarks to track relative capability over time as models improve.

The course's multimodal structure, combining code labs, live streams, white papers, and Discord, was chosen specifically because a book-format treatment of the same material would have been outdated before publication.
